terms.name: API Gateway
terms.tldr: A single front door for APIs that handles auth, rate limits, and routing.
terms.explainer: A reverse proxy that gives a fleet of backend services one front door, handling the cross-cutting concerns no single service should reimplement: terminating TLS, validating OAuth tokens and API keys, enforcing rate limits, and routing or versioning requests. Centralising authentication and throttling makes it a natural zero-trust enforcement point at the application edge, though pushing too much logic into it turns it into a bottleneck.

terms.name: BGP
terms.tldr: The routing protocol that stitches the internet's networks together.
terms.explainer: The routing protocol autonomous systems use to advertise which IP ranges they can reach and to choose paths between one another. Its choices follow commercial peering and transit policy rather than pure shortest-path, so one bad announcement can reroute traffic worldwide.

terms.name: BlueField
terms.tldr: NVIDIA's DPU: a ConnectX NIC fused with Arm cores and accelerators that runs the data center's networking, storage, and zero-trust security on the card.
terms.explainer: NVIDIA's DPU product line: each card pairs a ConnectX network ASIC with Arm cores running their own Linux, isolated from the host across PCIe, so infrastructure and security services run on the card itself. A hardware root of trust proves the card and its software are unmodified, IPsec/TLS run at line rate, and DOCA Argus reads host memory from the card to catch malware that host agents would miss. BlueField-3 (400 Gb/s) is the current production generation, with BlueField-4 announced for the AI-factory era.

terms.name: BOLA
terms.tldr: Swap the ID in an API request and read another user's object — because the server never checked it was yours.
terms.explainer: The most common API flaw: an endpoint authenticates the caller but never checks that the requested object belongs to them, so changing /orders/1001 to /orders/1002 returns someone else's record. The API-era name for IDOR, it tops the OWASP API Security Top 10 (API1:2023); the fix is a server-side ownership or policy check on every client-supplied identifier — unpredictable UUIDs raise the bar but are obscurity, not authorization.

source.label: OWASP API Security Top 10 — API1:2023 BOLA

terms.name: CASB
terms.tldr: A control point between users and SaaS apps that enforces cloud security policy.
terms.explainer: A control point between users and SaaS apps — inline proxy or API integration — that restores the visibility and policy enforcement the corporate network used to provide. It surfaces shadow IT, governs access and data flows, and applies data-loss-prevention controls, forming one of the core pillars of a SASE/SSE stack alongside secure web gateways and ZTNA.

source.label: NIST glossary — Cloud Access Security Broker

terms.name: Cilium / eBPF
terms.tldr: Programmable, in-kernel networking and security policy for Kubernetes.
terms.explainer: Builds on eBPF — sandboxed programs running safely inside the Linux kernel — to provide networking, observability, and security for Kubernetes at kernel speed rather than through userspace proxies. Because it enforces identity-aware network policy in the kernel, it implements microsegmentation efficiently and offers service-mesh-like connectivity without sidecars.

terms.name: CORS
terms.tldr: A browser rule that lets a server declare which other origins may read its responses.
terms.explainer: A browser-enforced mechanism that lets a server opt specific foreign origins into reading its responses via matching Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers, with a preflight check for non-simple requests. It only governs in-browser JavaScript and is no substitute for real authorization.

terms.name: CVE / CVSS
terms.tldr: The shared ID and 0–10 score that let everyone talk about the same flaw.
terms.explainer: A CVE identifier — like CVE-2021-44228 for Log4Shell — is the shared name, coordinated by MITRE, that lets vendors, scanners, and defenders point at the same flaw, while CVSS rates its severity from 0 to 10. NIST's National Vulnerability Database enriches CVE records with scores and affected-product data so teams can prioritise, but the number is guidance: real risk depends on whether the flaw is exploitable in your context.

terms.name: DHCP
terms.tldr: Automatically hands out IP addresses to devices joining a network.
terms.explainer: Automatically configures a newly connected device with its IP address, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS servers through a short Discover-Offer-Request-Acknowledge exchange. Addresses are handed out as time-limited leases, which is why devices 'just work' on a new network.

terms.name: Django
terms.tldr: A batteries-included Python web framework for building sites fast.
terms.explainer: A batteries-included Python web framework shipping an ORM, admin, auth, and routing, following a model-template-view pattern. Its philosophy is to handle the plumbing so developers can focus on the application.

terms.name: DLP
terms.tldr: Controls that detect and stop sensitive data from leaving where it should not.
terms.explainer: The set of controls that keep sensitive information — card numbers, health records, source code, secrets — from leaving via email, uploads, USB, or chat, inspecting content against patterns and classifications then warning, blocking, or encrypting on a policy breach. Operating at endpoints, on the network, and in the cloud via CASB, it addresses the Data pillar of zero trust, and is only as good as the data classification beneath it.

terms.name: DNS
terms.tldr: The internet's phonebook: turns human names into IP addresses.
terms.explainer: Resolves human-readable names like example.com into the IP addresses machines route to, with a resolver walking the root → TLD → authoritative hierarchy and caching answers for speed. It is also a powerful control point for security and filtering.

terms.name: DOCA
terms.tldr: The SDK for BlueField — 'what CUDA is to the GPU, DOCA is to the DPU' — with libraries and services to offload networking, storage, and zero-trust security.
terms.explainer: NVIDIA's software framework for programming BlueField DPUs and SuperNICs: hardware drivers at the base, C/C++ libraries in the middle, and containerized microservices on top, exposed through standard interfaces such as DPDK and SPDK. Its zero-trust components run on the DPU's isolated cores — DOCA Argus inspects the host's live memory and processes agentlessly, App Shield monitors process integrity, and IPsec/TLS gateways encrypt at wire speed — and the common interface keeps that software portable across BlueField generations.

terms.name: Docker
terms.tldr: Packages an app and its dependencies into a portable container.
terms.explainer: Packages an application with its dependencies into a lightweight, isolated container that shares the host kernel instead of booting a full VM, built from cacheable layered images that run identically anywhere. It is the deployment unit most modern platforms, including Kubernetes, build on.

terms.name: DPU
terms.tldr: A programmable processor on the network card that offloads networking, storage, and security from the host — and runs them in its own isolated trust domain.
terms.explainer: A system-on-chip on the network card that fuses a high-speed NIC with its own general-purpose cores (typically Arm) and accelerators, running packet switching, encryption, storage, and firewalling in an operating system the host cannot see or control. Because traffic passes through the DPU before it reaches the host, enforcement survives even a fully compromised host OS — a hardware zero-trust enforcement point on every server. NVIDIA BlueField, AWS Nitro, Intel IPU, and AMD Pensando are the leading implementations.

terms.name: EDR / XDR
terms.tldr: Agents that watch endpoints for malicious behaviour — and feed device-trust signals.
terms.explainer: A lightweight agent on laptops and servers continuously records process, file, and network activity, flags behaviour matching known attack patterns, and lets responders isolate or roll back a compromised host; XDR widens that lens across email, identity, and cloud so a multi-stage attack reads as one incident. Its live health verdict also becomes the Devices-pillar posture signal a zero-trust policy engine weighs, so a failing device can be denied even with valid credentials.

terms.name: Firewall
terms.tldr: Filters network traffic against a set of allow and deny rules.
terms.explainer: Sits at a network boundary and allows or blocks traffic by rules based on addresses, ports, and increasingly application and identity. Stateful and next-generation variants track connections and inspect deeper, forming the classic first line of defence between trusted and untrusted zones.

terms.name: gRPC
terms.tldr: A fast, contract-first RPC framework using Protocol Buffers over HTTP/2.
terms.explainer: A contract-first RPC framework where a .proto file generates client and server code, letting services call each other like local functions. It uses compact binary Protocol Buffers over HTTP/2 for low-latency multiplexed streaming, favoured for internal microservice traffic where REST/JSON is too chatty.

terms.name: HTTP / HTTPS
terms.tldr: The request/response protocol of the web; HTTPS is HTTP wrapped in TLS.
terms.explainer: The stateless request/response protocol browsers and APIs use to fetch and submit resources via methods like GET and POST, with cookies or tokens carrying context between requests. HTTPS is the very same protocol running inside a TLS-encrypted channel.

terms.name: Identity Provider
terms.tldr: The trusted service that authenticates users and vouches for who they are.
terms.explainer: The central authority that authenticates users and issues the SAML/OAuth/OIDC assertions or tokens applications trust, so individual apps need not manage passwords. It is the hub of single sign-on and the anchor of identity in zero-trust designs.

terms.name: IDS / IPS
terms.tldr: Sensors that spot attacks in network traffic — and optionally block them inline.
terms.explainer: Inspects network or host activity for attack signatures, protocol anomalies, or deviations from a baseline; an IDS raises an alert, while an IPS sits inline and can drop or reset the malicious connection. Open-source engines like Snort and Suricata popularised the signature model, feeding a SIEM for correlation and complementing endpoint EDR by watching the wire — at the cost of false positives, and the risk of blocking legitimate traffic inline.

terms.name: IPsec
terms.tldr: Encrypts and authenticates IP traffic at the network layer.
terms.explainer: A suite that encrypts and integrity-protects traffic at the IP layer, so any application's packets are secured with no code changes. Transport mode protects host-to-host payloads while tunnel mode wraps whole packets between gateways — the basis of many site-to-site VPNs — with IKE handling key exchange.

terms.name: JWT
terms.tldr: A signed, self-contained token that carries identity claims.
terms.explainer: A signed, self-contained token carrying identity claims in three base64url parts (header, payload, signature) that any holder can verify without a database lookup, making it popular for stateless sessions and API access. Payloads are readable, not encrypted, so secrets do not belong in them.

terms.name: Kerberos
terms.tldr: Ticket-based authentication that proves identity without sending passwords over the wire.
terms.explainer: Lets a client prove its identity to many services using time-limited encrypted tickets instead of resending a password: after one authentication to a Key Distribution Center it receives a Ticket-Granting Ticket, then exchanges it for per-service tickets the targets can verify. It is the authentication half of Active Directory and the basis of Windows-domain single sign-on, and — relying on synchronised clocks — is notoriously sensitive to time drift.

terms.name: Kubernetes
terms.tldr: Automates deploying, scaling, and healing containerized apps across machines.
terms.explainer: A container orchestrator that runs workloads across a cluster: you declare desired state and its control plane continuously reconciles reality, rescheduling failures and scaling on demand. It turns a fleet of machines into one programmable platform, with the pod as its smallest unit.

terms.name: LDAP / Active Directory
terms.tldr: The directory that stores users, groups, and devices — and answers who is this?
terms.explainer: The protocol for querying and modifying a directory: a hierarchical database of users, groups, and devices, each with a distinguished name and attributes. Microsoft Active Directory is the dominant implementation, pairing an LDAP directory with Kerberos to become the identity backbone of most enterprises and the source of truth that RADIUS, SAML, and SCIM build on — which also makes it a prime target.

terms.name: Load Balancer
terms.tldr: Spreads incoming traffic across many backend servers.
terms.explainer: Distributes incoming requests across a pool of backend servers by round-robin, load, health, or content, automatically removing unhealthy backends to improve capacity and resilience. Operating at layer 4 or layer 7, it is also a natural place to terminate TLS.

terms.name: MFA / Passkeys
terms.tldr: Proving identity with more than a password — and passkeys that cannot be phished.
terms.explainer: Requires two or more independent proofs of identity — something you know, have, or are — so a stolen password alone is not enough; but SMS and TOTP codes can still be phished into a fake site. FIDO2 and WebAuthn close that gap by binding a public-key challenge to the real site's origin so the credential cannot be replayed, and passkeys are the consumer-friendly, password-replacing form — the strongest signal that the human is who they claim to be.

terms.name: Microsegmentation
terms.tldr: Divides a network into tiny zones, each with its own access policy.
terms.explainer: Pushes network isolation down to individual workloads, governing traffic between them with fine-grained per-segment policy instead of one flat internal network. By permitting only the flows each workload needs, it contains lateral movement if a host is compromised — a practical zero-trust building block.

source.label: NIST SP 800-215 — Secure Enterprise Network Landscape

terms.name: MQTT
terms.fullName: MQTT (historically 'MQ Telemetry Transport' — no longer an acronym)
terms.tldr: A lightweight publish/subscribe protocol for IoT and constrained devices.
terms.explainer: A lightweight publish/subscribe protocol for low-bandwidth or unreliable networks, where clients publish to named topics on a central broker and others subscribe, decoupling senders from receivers. Its tiny footprint and tiered quality-of-service make it the default for IoT sensors and telemetry.

terms.name: mTLS
terms.tldr: TLS where both client and server prove identity with certificates.
terms.explainer: Mutual TLS extends ordinary TLS so the client also presents a certificate, giving strong two-way authentication without passwords before any data flows. It is a cornerstone of zero-trust service-to-service communication and service meshes, where every workload carries its own cryptographic identity.

terms.name: NAT
terms.tldr: Lets many private devices share one public IP address.
terms.explainer: Rewrites the source/destination IP and port of packets crossing a boundary, typically mapping many private addresses onto one public IP to conserve IPv4 space while incidentally hiding internal structure. A translation table ensures replies return to the correct internal host.

terms.name: nDLP
terms.tldr: The network arm of DLP — inspects data in motion at egress and blocks sensitive content before it leaves.
terms.explainer: The network-enforced arm of DLP: an inline inspection point — a forward proxy, secure web gateway, or cloud SSE service — that examines data in motion leaving via web uploads, email, and cloud sync, matching content against patterns and classifications, then warning, blocking, or quarantining on a policy breach. Because most egress traffic is encrypted it depends on TLS interception to see payloads, and in SASE architectures it shares the cloud enforcement point with CASB and ZTNA. It covers what endpoint agents cannot — unmanaged devices and server egress — while endpoint DLP handles USB, clipboard, and print.

source.label: IBM — What is data loss prevention (DLP)?

terms.name: NIST SP 800-207
terms.tldr: The reference document that defines Zero Trust and its policy components.
terms.explainer: NIST Special Publication 800-207 (2020) is the vendor-neutral reference architecture that turned zero trust from a slogan into a defined model, setting out seven tenets and naming the logical components that enforce them — the Policy Engine and Policy Administrator behind the PDP, and the Policy Enforcement Point in the data path. It is the spec the PEP/PDP terms here come from, and the basis CISA's five-pillar Zero Trust Maturity Model builds on.

terms.name: OAuth 2.0
terms.tldr: Lets an app access your data on another service without your password.
terms.explainer: An authorization framework that issues an app a scoped, revocable access token instead of your credentials: you log in, consent, and the app calls the API on your behalf. It powers 'Sign in with…' delegation and is about authorization, not authentication.

terms.name: OBO / Token Exchange
terms.tldr: Lets a middle service call the next API as the user who called it, by swapping tokens — identity flows through the chain, not service accounts.
terms.explainer: Keeps identity intact across service chains: instead of calling downstream APIs with its own all-powerful service account, a middle tier hands the caller's token back to the identity provider and exchanges it for a new one — audience-scoped to the next hop, naming the original user as subject and the service as actor. Standardised as OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange (RFC 8693) and shipped as Microsoft Entra's On-Behalf-Of flow, it distinguishes delegation from impersonation and defeats the confused deputy: a privileged intermediary tricked into misusing its authority. Each hop can then authorize the person actually asking — least privilege past the front door.

terms.name: OIDC
terms.tldr: An identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 that tells an app who the user is.
terms.explainer: An identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 that adds authentication: alongside the access token, the provider issues a signed ID Token (a JWT) describing who the user is. Requesting the openid scope turns a plain OAuth exchange into OIDC, the modern JSON successor to SAML for SSO.

terms.name: OPA
terms.tldr: A general-purpose policy engine that answers allow / deny from code, not config.
terms.explainer: A CNCF-graduated engine that decouples authorization from application code: a service sends JSON context asking whether subject X may do action Y on resource Z, and OPA evaluates rules written in its declarative Rego language to return a structured decision. Because one engine serves microservices, Kubernetes admission, CI, and API gateways, authorization becomes version-controlled policy-as-code — the canonical software implementation of a zero-trust Policy Decision Point.

terms.name: OpenZiti
terms.tldr: An open-source overlay that gives every app an identity and a default-deny, encrypted path — a zero-trust VPN replacement.
terms.explainer: An open-source zero-trust overlay where every app, device, or service carries an X.509 identity and a connection is allowed only when policy grants that identity access to a named service. Services are 'dark' — no inbound ports, mutually authenticated with mTLS — so unlike a VPN, being on the network grants nothing by itself.

terms.name: Open Virtual Network
terms.fullName: OVN — SDN control plane for OVS
terms.tldr: An SDN control plane on top of Open vSwitch that compiles logical switches, routers, and ACLs into the flows each host runs — the virtual network behind OpenStack and Kubernetes.
terms.explainer: The SDN control plane on top of Open vSwitch: you declare logical switches, routers, and ACLs in its northbound database, and OVN compiles that intent into the OpenFlow rules every OVS instance runs, spanning hosts as one overlay network. Per-port ACLs and a stateful distributed firewall enforce east-west isolation in the data plane itself — the microsegmentation engine behind OpenStack Neutron and the ovn-kubernetes CNI.

terms.name: Open vSwitch
terms.tldr: The open-source programmable switch inside the hypervisor — it wires VMs and containers to the network and forwards their packets by flow rules.
terms.explainer: A production-grade, multilayer virtual switch that runs in the hypervisor or host and connects VMs, containers, and the physical NIC. Unlike a plain Linux bridge it is programmable — controllers install OpenFlow rules into its flow tables and OVSDB configures its bridges — so forwarding, tunnelling (VXLAN/Geneve), and filtering follow policy, and the same flows can be offloaded to a SmartNIC or DPU at line rate. It forwards packets on its own but needs a control plane like OVN to decide what the flows should be.

terms.name: OWASP Top 10
terms.tldr: The community's ranked list of the most critical web application risks.
terms.explainer: A non-profit community producing free, vendor-neutral application-security resources, best known for the Top 10: a periodically updated, ranked list of the most critical web-application risks drawn from real-world data. Developers code against it, WAFs ship rule sets named after it, and the 2025 edition keeps Broken Access Control at number one while adding Software Supply Chain Failures (expanded from 2021's Vulnerable and Outdated Components) — but it is a floor for awareness, not a complete security programme.

terms.name: PAC File
terms.tldr: A snippet of JavaScript the browser runs for every URL to decide: go direct, or through which proxy?
terms.explainer: A small JavaScript file exporting one function — FindProxyForURL(url, host) — that the browser calls for every request to decide: go direct, or through which proxy? Rules match on hostname, domain, or IP range, so internal traffic goes direct while everything else is steered into a forward proxy or Secure Web Gateway — the classic agentless on-ramp into SASE inspection. Browsers locate it via a configured URL or WPAD discovery (DHCP/DNS), a path attackers have abused, so modern browsers strip HTTPS paths before calling the script.

terms.name: PAM
terms.tldr: Tight control over the powerful admin accounts attackers most want.
terms.explainer: Wraps the handful of privileged accounts — domain admins, root, cloud owners — that can do catastrophic damage in extra control: vaulting their credentials, brokering sessions through a gateway that records keystrokes, and increasingly granting access just-in-time so privileges are elevated for one task and a short window, then revoked. A higher-assurance complement to ordinary identity management, it shrinks the standing attack surface and supplies the audit trail regulators expect.

terms.name: PEP / PDP
terms.fullName: Policy Enforcement Point / Policy Decision Point
terms.tldr: The enforce-here, decide-there split at the heart of access control.
terms.explainer: The access-control pattern that splits enforcement from decision: the Policy Enforcement Point intercepts each request in-path while the Policy Decision Point evaluates the rules and returns allow or deny. Centralising the decision lets one policy brain govern many enforcement points — the control core of zero trust.

terms.name: PKCE
terms.tldr: Stops a stolen OAuth authorization code from being redeemed by anyone but the app that began the flow.
terms.explainer: Hardens OAuth's authorization-code flow against interception: the client sends only the hash of a random secret (code_verifier) when starting login and must present the original to redeem tokens, making a stolen code useless. Born for mobile and single-page apps, it is now recommended for all clients and mandatory in the OAuth 2.1 draft.

terms.name: PKI
terms.tldr: The certificate-and-trust system that lets you prove who owns a public key.
terms.explainer: The machinery that binds a public key to an identity and lets a stranger trust that binding: a Certificate Authority signs X.509 certificates, and anyone holding the CA's certificate can verify the signature up a chain to a trusted root. It is the foundation under TLS, mTLS, code signing, and SPIFFE — every browser padlock is a PKI trust decision — with ACME automating issuance at scale; get the root of trust wrong and everything above it falls.

terms.name: Post-Quantum Cryptography
terms.tldr: New algorithms designed to survive attack by a large quantum computer.
terms.explainer: Replaces today's RSA and elliptic-curve public-key crypto — breakable by a large quantum computer running Shor's algorithm — with algorithms built on problems, chiefly structured lattices, that resist both classical and quantum attack. NIST finalised the first standards in 2024 (ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA); the urgency is 'harvest now, decrypt later', so long-lived secrets need protecting today, with rollout happening as a hybrid layer alongside classical algorithms in TLS.

terms.name: QUIC
terms.fullName: QUIC (a name, not an acronym — RFC 9000)
terms.tldr: A fast, always-encrypted transport that runs over UDP and folds in the work of TCP plus TLS.
terms.explainer: A UDP-based transport that folds connection setup, TLS 1.3 encryption, and multiplexed streams into one handshake, so a connection is usually ready in a single round trip (zero when resuming). Independent streams remove TCP's head-of-line blocking, and it is the always-encrypted foundation of HTTP/3.

terms.name: RADIUS
terms.tldr: A protocol for centralized authentication, authorization, and accounting.
terms.explainer: Centralises network access control: when you join Wi-Fi, a VPN, or a switch port, the access device asks a RADIUS server to authenticate you, decide what you may do, and log the session. It is the long-standing AAA backbone separating the access device from the identity store.

terms.name: RBAC & ABAC
terms.tldr: Two models for deciding who may do what — by role, or by attributes and context.
terms.explainer: The two dominant models for deciding what an authenticated user may do. Role-Based Access Control groups permissions into roles and assigns people to them — simple but coarse and prone to role explosion — while Attribute-Based Access Control evaluates rules over subject, resource, action, and environment for fine-grained, context-aware decisions; zero trust leans toward ABAC because trust should follow live context, not a static role.

source.label: NIST — Role Based Access Control project

terms.name: React
terms.tldr: A JavaScript library for building user interfaces from composable components.
terms.explainer: A JavaScript library for building UIs as a tree of reusable components whose output is a function of their state and props. When state changes it re-renders efficiently by diffing a virtual DOM and patching only what changed, popularising the declarative front-end model.

terms.name: SAML
terms.tldr: An XML standard for single sign-on between identity and service providers.
terms.explainer: An XML standard for enterprise single sign-on where an identity provider authenticates the user and issues a digitally signed assertion that service providers trust, so one login unlocks many apps. It predates OAuth/OIDC but remains widespread in corporate and SaaS access.

terms.name: SASE
terms.tldr: Delivers networking and security together from the cloud edge, near the user.
terms.explainer: A cloud-delivered model converging wide-area networking with security services — secure web gateway, CASB, firewall-as-a-service, and ZTNA — enforced at points of presence near the user instead of backhauling traffic to a datacentre. It is how many organisations now operate zero trust.

source.label: NIST glossary — Secure Access Service Edge

terms.name: SBC
terms.tldr: The firewall for voice: polices, encrypts, and repairs SIP calls at the border between a phone system and the outside world.
terms.explainer: The border element for real-time voice and video: a back-to-back user agent that terminates each SIP session and re-originates it, hiding internal topology, policing signalling against toll fraud and DoS, encrypting calls with TLS and SRTP, fixing SIP's notorious NAT-traversal problems, and translating between vendor dialects and codecs. It does for telephony what an API gateway does for APIs — one controlled front door — sitting wherever an enterprise phone system meets a SIP trunk or Microsoft Teams Direct Routing. As a deliberate man-in-the-middle for every call it is a prime target itself, and modern deployments harden and monitor it like any other policy enforcement point.

terms.name: SBOM & SLSA
terms.tldr: Knowing exactly what is in your software, and proving how it was built.
terms.explainer: Modern apps are assembled from hundreds of dependencies, so attackers target the supply chain rather than the app itself. A Software Bill of Materials is a machine-readable inventory (CycloneDX or SPDX) of every component and version, so the next Log4Shell can be answered in seconds, while SLSA raises how tamper-resistant the build pipeline is and emits signed provenance — together making the supply chain auditable, now its own category in the OWASP Top 10:2025.

terms.name: SCIM
terms.tldr: Automates creating and removing user accounts across apps when people join or leave.
terms.explainer: A REST/JSON standard that syncs user accounts between an identity provider and connected apps, pushing joiners, movers, and leavers via CRUD on /Users and /Groups. Where SAML and OIDC handle login, SCIM handles the account lifecycle around it — closing the gap left by orphaned accounts after offboarding.

terms.name: SD-WAN
terms.tldr: Software steers branch traffic across multiple links by policy, not fixed routing.
terms.explainer: Lays a software overlay across a branch's mixed transports (MPLS, broadband, cellular) while a central controller steers each application over the best path by policy and live link quality. It replaces device-by-device WAN config with centralised management and is often paired with SASE for security.

terms.name: Service Mesh
terms.tldr: A networking layer that gives every service identity, mTLS, and traffic control for free.
terms.explainer: Moves the cross-cutting concerns of service-to-service traffic — mutual TLS, retries, timeouts, load balancing, observability — out of application code and into a dedicated layer: a sidecar proxy (commonly Envoy) beside each workload forms the data plane, configured by a control plane like Istio or Linkerd. Every call becomes a mutually authenticated, policy-checked hop, delivering encrypted east-west traffic and workload identity with no app changes — at the cost of operational complexity and an extra hop.

terms.name: SIEM
terms.tldr: Collects logs from everywhere and correlates them into security alerts.
terms.explainer: Ingests and normalises logs from across the estate — firewalls, servers, apps, cloud — then correlates them to surface suspicious patterns as prioritised alerts for a security operations centre. Modern platforms add analytics and automated response and help satisfy audit requirements.

terms.name: Sigstore
terms.tldr: Easy, keyless signing of software artifacts backed by a public transparency log.
terms.explainer: Removes the long-lived signing key that, once leaked, forges trust: its Cosign tool signs an artifact with a short-lived certificate that Fulcio issues against an OIDC identity, so you sign as who you are rather than as a key you must hoard. Every signature is recorded in Rekor, a public append-only transparency log, making verifiable provenance practical at ecosystem scale and pairing naturally with SBOMs and SLSA.

terms.name: SOAR
terms.tldr: Automation that turns security alerts into executed response playbooks.
terms.explainer: Automates the repetitive triage that drowns security teams by connecting the tools an analyst would otherwise click through — SIEM, EDR, threat intelligence, ticketing, firewalls — and running codified playbooks that enrich an alert, judge whether it is real, and act: isolate a host, disable an account, block an IP. Sitting atop SIEM detection in a modern SOC, it cuts response from hours to seconds, with destructive steps gated behind human approval lest it automate a wrong decision at machine speed.

terms.name: SPIFFE / SPIRE
terms.fullName: Secure Production Identity Framework For Everyone
terms.tldr: A standard and engine for giving workloads cryptographic identities.
terms.explainer: Defines a universal workload identity (the SPIFFE ID) and a provable document for it (the SVID, an X.509 certificate or JWT), with SPIRE as the reference engine that attests workloads and issues short-lived identities. Together they let services authenticate with mTLS instead of shared secrets — foundational zero-trust plumbing.

terms.name: SSH
terms.tldr: An encrypted channel for remote login and command execution.
terms.explainer: Provides an authenticated, encrypted connection to a remote machine — a safe replacement for cleartext remote access — using passwords or, preferably, public-key pairs. It secures interactive shells, file transfer, and port forwarding, and is the everyday tool for administering servers.

terms.name: SuperNIC
terms.tldr: A network accelerator tuned for AI: line-rate GPU-to-GPU Ethernet (RoCE) with the congestion control huge training clusters need — the DPU's performance-focused sibling.
terms.explainer: NVIDIA's network accelerator for the east-west, GPU-to-GPU traffic of AI clusters: line-rate RDMA over Converged Ethernet (RoCE) with the adaptive routing and congestion control that keep thousands of GPUs fed, as a pillar of the Spectrum-X Ethernet platform. Two structurally different products share the name — the BlueField-3 SuperNIC, a slimmed-down DPU with eight Arm cores, and the ConnectX-8/9 SuperNICs, pure adapters with no separate OS. Its remit is performance over policy: traffic is still encrypted in flight, but the DPU's host-introspection services don't apply.

terms.name: Tailscale
terms.tldr: A zero-config mesh VPN that connects your devices directly over WireGuard.
terms.explainer: Instead of backhauling every device through a central gateway, builds a flat mesh in which devices connect directly over encrypted WireGuard tunnels; a coordination server handles authentication via your existing identity provider and key distribution, then steps out of the data path, with NAT traversal and DERP relays making peer-to-peer work behind firewalls. Access follows identity and ACLs rather than network location, making this tailnet a practical on-ramp to zero-trust networking and a peer to OpenZiti.

terms.name: TCP/IP
terms.tldr: The layered addressing-and-delivery system that moves data across the internet.
terms.explainer: The foundational layered suite that interconnects dissimilar networks: IP addresses and routes packets, TCP adds reliable ordered delivery (with UDP as the lightweight alternative), organised into link, internet, transport, and application layers. Almost every other protocol here runs on top of it.

terms.name: TLS
terms.tldr: Encrypts data in transit and authenticates the server you are talking to.
terms.explainer: The successor to SSL, giving any TCP connection confidentiality, integrity, and server authentication: a handshake uses the server's certificate and public-key crypto to agree session keys, then encrypts traffic with fast symmetric ciphers. It underpins HTTPS, secure email, and many VPNs; mTLS extends it to both sides.

terms.name: TPM & Secure Boot
terms.tldr: A hardware chip that protects keys and proves a machine booted clean.
terms.explainer: A tamper-resistant chip that generates and stores cryptographic keys so they never leave the hardware, and measures the software loaded at boot; Secure Boot verifies each startup stage against trusted signatures while measured boot records hashes into the chip's registers. A remote server can then ask the TPM to attest that the device booted into a known-good state — a hardware root of trust underpinning disk-encryption keys, device identity, and the device-posture checks zero trust relies on.

terms.name: Secrets Management
terms.tldr: A central, audited vault for the passwords, keys, and tokens that machines need.
terms.explainer: Hard-coding credentials in config or source control leaks them, so a secrets manager such as HashiCorp Vault stores database passwords, API keys, and certificates centrally — encrypted, behind authentication and fine-grained policy — and releases them only to identities that prove they should have them. Its strongest pattern is dynamic secrets: short-lived credentials generated on demand and auto-revoked, every access logged, so in zero trust a workload (often via SPIFFE) fetches exactly what it needs, just in time.

terms.name: VLAN
terms.tldr: Splits one physical switch into several isolated logical networks.
terms.explainer: Partitions one physical network into separate broadcast domains by tagging Ethernet frames (802.1Q), so devices on different VLANs cannot talk directly without a router even on the same switch. It separates traffic like guest, voice, and server for better security, performance, and manageability.

terms.name: VPN
terms.tldr: An encrypted tunnel that extends a private network over the public internet.
terms.explainer: Builds an encrypted tunnel between a device or site and a network so traffic crosses the untrusted internet as if on a private link, used for remote access and to shield traffic on hostile networks. Its security and topology depend entirely on the underlying protocol — IPsec, WireGuard, or a TLS-based design.

terms.name: WAF
terms.tldr: A firewall that inspects HTTP to block web attacks a network firewall cannot see.
terms.explainer: Sits in front of an application and inspects HTTP at layer 7, blocking exploits — SQL injection, cross-site scripting, path traversal — that a packet-filtering network firewall, seeing only ports and addresses, would happily pass. It matches traffic against rule sets like the OWASP Core Rule Set and can virtually patch a known flaw by dropping the malicious pattern until the code is fixed, a control at the application edge that complements — but never replaces — secure code.

terms.name: CSRF / XSS / CSP
terms.fullName: Web App Attacks & Content Security Policy
terms.tldr: The two classic browser attacks — and the header that helps shut them down.
terms.explainer: The classic browser attacks and their defences: Cross-Site Scripting injects attacker script that runs with the victim's session, while Cross-Site Request Forgery tricks an authenticated browser into firing unwanted state-changing requests. They are countered by output escaping, anti-CSRF tokens and SameSite cookies, and a Content-Security-Policy header that whitelists what a page may load — defence in depth, since none suffices alone.

terms.name: WebSockets
terms.tldr: Upgrades an HTTP connection into a persistent, two-way channel for live data.
terms.explainer: Upgrades an HTTP/1.1 request into a long-lived, full-duplex TCP connection (the server replies 101 Switching Protocols) so client and server can push lightweight frames at any time. That removes plain HTTP's turn-taking, powering chat, collaborative editing, and live feeds; use wss:// to wrap it in TLS.

terms.name: WebTransport
terms.fullName: Multiplexed streams and datagrams over HTTP/3
terms.tldr: A newer browser transport over HTTP/3 offering multiplexed streams and fire-and-forget datagrams.
terms.explainer: Gives browser code a low-latency session over HTTP/3 (and thus QUIC/UDP), mixing reliable streams with unreliable datagrams for uses like game state or live media. Inheriting QUIC's independent streams avoids the head-of-line blocking WebSockets suffer over TCP — an emerging alternative that reached cross-browser support in 2026.

terms.name: WireGuard
terms.tldr: A fast, modern, minimal VPN protocol built on current cryptography.
terms.explainer: A deliberately minimal, fast VPN protocol whose codebase is small enough to audit by hand, identifying peers by public keys and establishing low-overhead encrypted tunnels with quick handshakes. Shipping in the Linux kernel, it has become the modern default over heavier IPsec and OpenVPN.

terms.name: Zero Trust
terms.tldr: Trust nothing by default; verify every request regardless of network location.
terms.explainer: A security model that abandons the trusted internal network: every request is authenticated, authorized, and encrypted based on identity and context, whether it comes from inside or outside the old perimeter. It rests on strong identity, least-privilege access, and continuous verification.

terms.name: ZTNA
terms.tldr: The VPN replacement that grants access to single apps, never the whole network.
terms.explainer: Puts zero-trust principles on the wire and is the category replacing the traditional VPN: where a VPN drops an authenticated user onto the network to roam, ZTNA brokers access to one specific application at a time, re-verifying identity and device posture on every request and keeping everything else invisible. Apps connect outbound to the broker so there is no inbound port to attack — the software-defined-perimeter idea. Google's BeyondCorp was the influential early implementation, and ZTNA is now a core component of SASE/SSE.
